questions.question: Select three findings or laboratory results appropriate for suspecting DKA.
choices: A patient with diabetes or hyperglycemia has serum beta-hydroxybutyrate elevated to 3 mmol/L or higher
Hyperglycemia is accompanied by decreased pH or decreased bicarbonate
A patient taking an SGLT2 inhibitor has ketosis and metabolic acidosis even without marked hyperglycemia
Blood glucose is 600 mg/dL or higher with hyperosmolality, but ketosis and acidosis are not prominent
Hyperglycemia is present, but there is no acidosis and ketones are negative
questions.explanation: DKA is suspected when diabetes or hyperglycemia, ketosis, and metabolic acidosis are present together. SGLT2 inhibitor-associated DKA may present as euglycemic DKA without marked hyperglycemia. Blood glucose of 600 mg/dL or higher with elevated plasma osmolality and without prominent ketosis or acidosis is more suggestive of HHS.

questions.question: Select all correct statements regarding initial treatment of DKA/HHS.
choices: Start IV fluids with normal saline or an isotonic crystalloid at 500-1000 mL/h
An insulin bolus is not mandatory; treatment can be started with continuous IV infusion
In DKA, consider insulin administration at 0.1 units/kg/h
In HHS, consider insulin administration at 0.05 units/kg/h
If K <3.5 mmol/L, prioritize insulin administration over potassium replacement
questions.explanation: The pillars of initial DKA/HHS treatment are IV fluids, potassium management, insulin, and search for precipitating causes. In DKA, regular insulin at 0.1 units/kg/h is considered; in HHS, a lower dose such as 0.05 units/kg/h is considered. An insulin bolus is not mandatory. Starting insulin in the setting of hypokalemia can cause life-threatening arrhythmias due to intracellular potassium shift, so potassium replacement takes priority when K <3.5 mmol/L.

questions.question: Select three findings or clinical contexts appropriate for suspecting adrenal crisis.
choices: Unexplained shock or hypotension with poor response to IV fluids and vasopressors
History of long-term corticosteroid use with added stress such as infection or surgery
Acute illness accompanied by hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and hypoglycemia
Fever, tachycardia, diaphoresis, and hand tremor with low TSH and elevated FT4
Hyperglycemia, elevated serum ketones, and metabolic acidosis
questions.explanation: Adrenal crisis should be suspected with unexplained shock, hypotension poorly responsive to IV fluids and vasopressors, long-term corticosteroid use, stress such as infection or surgery, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, hypoglycemia, vomiting, abdominal pain, and severe malaise. Fever, tachycardia, diaphoresis, and hand tremor with low TSH and elevated FT4 suggest thyrotoxicosis or thyroid storm. Hyperglycemia, ketosis, and metabolic acidosis suggest DKA.

questions.question: A 42-year-old woman had been noted to have untreated Graves' disease. She is brought to the ED with pneumonia as a trigger. Temperature is 39.1°C, heart rate 148/min, blood pressure 156/72 mmHg, and SpO2 93%. She is restless and cannot converse coherently. FT4 is 4.8 ng/dL and TSH is <0.01 μIU/mL. Chest radiograph shows pneumonia but no pulmonary edema. Based on the Japan Thyroid Association diagnostic criteria, which assessment is most appropriate? Select one.
choices: Definite thyroid storm (TS1)
Suspected thyroid storm (TS2)
Thyrotoxicosis is present, but thyroid storm symptom criteria are not met
Because fever is caused by pneumonia, thyroid storm is excluded
Even with elevated FT4, thyroid storm cannot be diagnosed unless heart failure is present
questions.explanation: The diagnosis of thyroid storm requires thyrotoxicosis due to elevated FT3 or FT4 as a prerequisite. The Japan Thyroid Association criteria then assess central nervous system manifestations, fever of 38°C or higher, tachycardia of 130/min or higher, heart failure, and gastrointestinal/hepatic manifestations. This patient has elevated FT4, CNS symptoms manifested by restlessness, fever of 39.1°C, and heart rate 148/min, so she meets TS1. Infection can precipitate thyroid storm and does not by itself exclude the diagnosis.

questions.question: Select all correct statements regarding management of severe hypoglycemia.
choices: Do not force oral intake in patients with altered consciousness
Use intravenous dextrose or glucagon depending on the clinical situation
Check for causes such as insulin, sulfonylureas, poor oral intake, and reduced renal function
Continue observation after improvement because recurrent hypoglycemia can occur
Once the patient wakes after dextrose, involvement of long-acting insulin or sulfonylureas can be ruled out
questions.explanation: In severe hypoglycemia, forcing oral intake in a patient with altered consciousness carries a risk of aspiration. Intravenous dextrose or glucagon should be used for prompt correction, and causative medications, oral intake, renal function, infection, and other contributors should be checked. Recurrent hypoglycemia can occur with long-acting insulin, sulfonylureas, or reduced renal function, so observation is needed even after the patient wakes.

questions.question: A 25-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents with fever and vomiting. Blood glucose is 482 mg/dL, pH 7.18, HCO3- 9 mmol/L, K 5.2 mmol/L, and beta-hydroxybutyrate is elevated. Select three appropriate initial actions for DKA.
choices: Start normal saline or an isotonic crystalloid at 500-1000 mL/h
Prepare continuous IV regular insulin while rechecking potassium
Search for DKA precipitants such as infection or myocardial infarction
Because blood glucose exceeds 400 mg/dL, give 10 units of IV regular insulin before fluids
Because initial K is 5.2 mmol/L, give furosemide to lower potassium
questions.explanation: DKA management proceeds with IV fluids, potassium assessment, insulin, and search for precipitating causes. Even when initial potassium is high-normal or high, total body potassium is often depleted, and insulin, fluids, and improvement of acidosis can lower serum potassium. Furosemide is not usually given solely because K is 5.2 mmol/L.

questions.question: A patient with DKA has Na 132 mmol/L, Cl 96 mmol/L, and HCO3- 9 mmol/L. Select all correct statements regarding the anion gap and delta gap.
choices: AG is 17 and delta gap is 0, indicating normal anion gap metabolic acidosis
AG is 27, delta AG is 15, and delta HCO3- is 15, indicating high anion gap metabolic acidosis compatible with DKA
AG is 27, delta AG is 15, and delta HCO3- is 5, indicating that combined metabolic alkalosis is the main disorder
AG is 36, delta AG is 24, and delta HCO3- is 15, which is close to a diagnosis of HHS
If the delta gap is negative, consider concomitant normal anion gap metabolic acidosis
questions.explanation: AG = 132 - 96 - 9 = 27. If the normal AG is 12 and normal HCO3- is 24, delta AG is 15 and delta HCO3- is also 15, which is compatible with high anion gap metabolic acidosis due to DKA. A negative delta gap means the fall in HCO3- is greater than the rise in AG, suggesting concomitant normal anion gap metabolic acidosis such as diarrhea, renal tubular acidosis, or treatment-related hyperchloremic acidosis.

questions.question: Select all correct adult DKA diagnostic criteria based on the 2024 international consensus report.
choices: Blood glucose is 200 mg/dL or higher, or there is a prior history of diabetes
Serum beta-hydroxybutyrate is 3.0 mmol/L or higher, or urine ketones are 2+ or higher
pH is less than 7.30, or HCO3- is less than 18 mmol/L
If blood glucose is less than 250 mg/dL, DKA is excluded even if there is prior diabetes or ketonemia
Serum osmolality of 320 mOsm/kg or higher is required for the diagnosis of DKA
questions.explanation: Adult DKA is diagnosed using three elements: diabetes or hyperglycemia, ketonemia or ketonuria, and metabolic acidosis. The glucose criterion is blood glucose 200 mg/dL or higher or a prior history of diabetes. Ketosis is assessed using serum beta-hydroxybutyrate 3.0 mmol/L or higher or urine ketones 2+ or higher. Metabolic acidosis is assessed using pH <7.30 or HCO3- <18 mmol/L. Euglycemic DKA can occur with SGLT2 inhibitors and other settings. Serum osmolality of 320 mOsm/kg or higher is important for HHS, not required for DKA.

questions.question: A 50 kg patient with DKA is started on a continuous IV regular insulin infusion. Regular insulin 50 units is diluted with normal saline to a total volume of 50 mL. If the infusion is started at 0.1 units/kg/h, what is the infusion rate? Select one.
choices: 1 mL/h
2 mL/h
3 mL/h
5 mL/h
10 mL/h
questions.explanation: 50 units/50 mL equals 1 unit/mL. At 50 kg, 0.1 units/kg/h equals 5 units/h, so the infusion rate is 5 mL/h. In DKA, potassium should be checked first; if potassium is low, potassium replacement should precede insulin.

questions.question: During DKA treatment, blood glucose is 240 mg/dL, pH is 7.24, HCO3- is 13 mmol/L, and beta-hydroxybutyrate remains elevated. Which response is most appropriate? Select one.
choices: Continue insulin and add dextrose
Because blood glucose is below 250 mg/dL, switch to subcutaneous insulin
Wait for urine ketone results before deciding the insulin dose
Stop IV fluids and prioritize restarting oral intake
Add sodium bicarbonate because acidosis remains
questions.explanation: The goal of DKA treatment is resolution of ketoacidosis, not simply lowering blood glucose. When blood glucose falls below 250 mg/dL, add 5-10% dextrose to the IV fluids and continue insulin while avoiding hypoglycemia.

questions.question: A patient with type 2 diabetes taking an SGLT2 inhibitor presents with vomiting, malaise, and tachypnea. Blood glucose is 186 mg/dL, pH 7.21, HCO3- 11 mmol/L, and beta-hydroxybutyrate 5.4 mmol/L. Select all appropriate actions.
choices: Suspect euglycemic DKA
Stop the SGLT2 inhibitor
Treat with IV fluids, potassium management, and insulin
Consider early dextrose coadministration because blood glucose is relatively low
Treat as HHS with fluids alone and no insulin
questions.explanation: SGLT2 inhibitor-associated DKA may occur without marked hyperglycemia. Treatment should proceed as DKA, and early dextrose coadministration should be considered to prevent hypoglycemia while continuing insulin.

questions.question: During DKA treatment, blood glucose has fallen to 250 mg/dL or lower. Select all correct subsequent management principles.
choices: Until beta-hydroxybutyrate is <0.6 mmol/L and either pH is >=7.3 or HCO3- is >=18 mmol/L, continue continuous IV insulin with dextrose
Transition to subcutaneous insulin when acidosis and ketosis have improved and enteral nutrition is possible
After starting subcutaneous insulin, overlap it with the IV insulin infusion for about 1-2 hours
Adjust total daily subcutaneous insulin dose using roughly 0.3-0.6 units/kg/day as a guide
Switch to oral glucose-lowering medication once blood glucose reaches 250 mg/dL or lower
questions.explanation: Blood glucose of 250 mg/dL or lower does not mean DKA treatment is complete. The 2024 international consensus defines DKA resolution as plasma ketone <0.6 mmol/L and venous pH >=7.3 or bicarbonate >=18 mmol/L. If ketosis or acidosis remains, continue insulin with dextrose. Transition to subcutaneous insulin when acidosis and ketosis have improved and enteral nutrition is possible. Overlap subcutaneous insulin with IV insulin for about 1-2 hours. The subcutaneous insulin dose should be adjusted using body weight, preadmission insulin dose, and IV insulin requirements.

questions.question: A patient with DKA has blood glucose 620 mg/dL and measured Na 128 mmol/L. If Na is corrected by 2.4 mmol/L for each 100 mg/dL of glucose above 100 mg/dL, which corrected Na is closest? Select one.
choices: 132 mmol/L
136 mmol/L
141 mmol/L
146 mmol/L
151 mmol/L
questions.explanation: In hyperglycemia, movement of water into the extracellular space causes dilutional lowering of measured sodium. With glucose 620 mg/dL, the amount above 100 mg/dL is 520 mg/dL. Using a correction factor of 2.4 mmol/L gives 128 + 2.4 x 5.2 = 140.5 mmol/L, approximately 141 mmol/L. Some protocols use a correction factor of 1.6, which would give about 136 mmol/L, so interpretation should follow the local protocol.

questions.question: Select all criteria appropriate for the diagnosis of HHS based on the 2024 international consensus report.
choices: Blood glucose 600 mg/dL or higher
Effective serum osmolality >300 mOsm/kg, or total serum osmolality >320 mOsm/kg
Beta-hydroxybutyrate <3.0 mmol/L, or urine ketones less than 2+
pH 7.30 or higher and HCO3- 15 mmol/L or higher
Beta-hydroxybutyrate 3.0 mmol/L or higher, or HCO3- less than 18 mmol/L
questions.explanation: HHS is characterized by marked hyperglycemia, increased serum osmolality, absence of significant ketonemia, and absence of acidosis. The 2024 international consensus lists glucose >=600 mg/dL, effective serum osmolality >300 mOsm/kg or total serum osmolality >320 mOsm/kg, beta-hydroxybutyrate <3.0 mmol/L or urine ketones less than 2+, and pH >=7.30 with HCO3- >=15 mmol/L. Beta-hydroxybutyrate >=3.0 mmol/L or HCO3- <18 mmol/L suggests DKA or mixed DKA/HHS.

questions.question: Select three correct statements regarding initial medications for thyroid storm.
choices: Give methimazole 60 mg/day orally; if oral administration is difficult, give methimazole 30 mg/day intravenously
Give Lugol's solution 4-8 drops every 6-8 hours orally or via gastric tube after antithyroid drug administration
Give hydrocortisone 100-300 mg IV initially, followed by 100 mg every 8 hours
For tachycardia, give propranolol 40 mg IV regardless of heart failure or shock
For fever, give aspirin as the first-choice antipyretic
questions.explanation: Thyroid storm treatment combines antithyroid drugs, inorganic iodine, corticosteroids, beta-blockers, antipyretics, IV fluids, cooling, and other supportive care. For thyroid storm due to Graves' disease, methimazole 60 mg/day orally or methimazole 30 mg/day IV when oral administration is difficult may be used. Inorganic iodine is added after antithyroid drug administration to suppress thyroid hormone release. With 2% Lugol's solution, 4-8 drops every 6-8 hours orally or through a gastric tube is used. Hydrocortisone is given as 100-300 mg IV initially, followed by 100 mg every 8 hours. Beta-blockers are useful for tachycardia but should be selected cautiously in heart failure or shock. Aspirin can increase free thyroid hormone by affecting protein binding, so acetaminophen is used as the antipyretic.

questions.question: Which preparation method for 3% saline is correct? Select one.
choices: Normal saline 400 mL + 10% NaCl 120 mL
Normal saline 500 mL + 10% NaCl 20 mL
Normal saline 100 mL + 10% NaCl 120 mL
5% dextrose 400 mL + 10% NaCl 120 mL
Normal saline 1000 mL + 10% NaCl 120 mL
questions.explanation: Discarding 100 mL from a 500 mL bag of normal saline to leave 400 mL, then adding 120 mL of 10% NaCl, creates approximately 3% saline. In a patient around 50 kg, an infusion rate of about 25 mL/h is expected to raise serum sodium by roughly 0.3-0.5 mEq/L/h. Check electrolytes every 2-4 hours during correction and avoid overcorrection.

questions.question: Which statement about KCl replacement through a peripheral vein is correct? Select one.
choices: Maximum concentration 40 mEq/L, maximum rate 20 mEq/h
Maximum concentration 40 mEq/L, maximum rate 40 mEq/h
Maximum concentration 80 mEq/L, maximum rate 40 mEq/h
Maximum concentration 20 mEq/L, maximum rate 20 mEq/h
No concentration limit, maximum rate 20 mEq/h
questions.explanation: For KCl replacement through a peripheral vein, remember maximum concentration 40 mEq/L and maximum rate 20 mEq/h as the basic limit. Administration above 10 mEq/h should be performed with strict monitoring such as ECG monitoring. KCl must be given with an infusion pump, and rapid administration should be avoided.

questions.question: Which statement about calcium supplementation is incorrect? Select one.
choices: Administer calcium for QRS widening due to hyperkalemia
Administer calcium for tetany due to hypocalcemia
Massive transfusion can cause hypocalcemia
In hyperkalemia, the purpose is myocardial membrane stabilization
Rapidly administered 10 mL of calcium gluconate for hypocalcemia
questions.explanation: Calcium supplementation for hypocalcemia should not be given rapidly; it should be administered slowly through reliable venous access. Calcium gluconate 8.5% 10 mL contains about 3.9 mEq of calcium. Massive transfusion can cause hypocalcemia due to citrate. In hyperkalemia with ECG changes, calcium is given to stabilize the myocardial membrane, not to lower the serum potassium level.

questions.question: Select all correct indications and administration methods for magnesium sulfate.
choices: TdP: magnesium sulfate 2 g IV over 5-10 minutes
Eclamptic seizure: magnesium sulfate 4 g IV over at least 20 minutes
Severe asthma attack: magnesium sulfate 2 g IV over about 20 minutes
Hypomagnesemia: dilute 20 mL of magnesium sulfate correction solution in 100 mL normal saline and infuse over 2 hours
Atrioventricular block: magnesium sulfate 2 g IV over 20 minutes
questions.explanation: For torsades de pointes, magnesium sulfate 2 g IV is used as acute treatment. For eclamptic seizure, a regimen of magnesium sulfate 4 g IV over at least 20 minutes may be used; with a 1 mEq/mL magnesium sulfate correction solution, this corresponds to 32 mL. For severe asthma exacerbation or severe asthma not responding to initial therapy, magnesium sulfate 2 g IV over about 20 minutes may be used; with a 1 mEq/mL correction solution, 16 mL is about 2 g. For hypomagnesemia, one method is to dilute 20 mL of magnesium sulfate correction solution in 100 mL normal saline and infuse over 2 hours. Magnesium sulfate 2 g IV is not standard treatment for atrioventricular block.

questions.question: Which statement about phosphate replacement is correct? Select one.
choices: Administer sodium phosphate 20 mL + normal saline 250 mL over 6 hours
Rapidly inject sodium phosphate 20 mL intravenously
Administer sodium phosphate 20 mL daily for hypocalcemia
One packet of Hosribbon contains 10 mg of phosphorus
In malnourished patients, no inpatient replacement is needed if serum phosphorus is normal on arrival
questions.explanation: One method is sodium phosphate correction solution 0.5 mmol/mL, 20 mL, diluted in 250 mL normal saline and administered over 6 hours. Rapid administration carries risks such as hypocalcemia, calcium phosphate precipitation, and renal dysfunction, so phosphate is generally given slowly. Hosribbon combination granules contain 100 mg of phosphorus in one 0.48 g packet. Oral replacement is an option if the patient can take medications enterally. In malnourished patients, refeeding syndrome can reveal hypophosphatemia after admission, so repeat testing is needed.
